b' Money Stuff
Programming note: Money Stuff will be off tomorrow, back on Thursday.In March 2022, Barclays Plc%3Cp%3EI%20am%20just%20going%20to%20call%20t

Programming note: Money Stuff will be off tomorrow, back on Thursday.

Barclays!

In March 2022, Barclays Plc [1] discovered an oopsie involving a fairly technical detail of US securities law. When a\xc2\xa0company sells securities to the public, in the US, it has to register those securities, filing a registration statement with the US Securities and Exchange Commission that says what kinds and amounts of securities it plans to sell. This is very important. If you sell securities\xc2\xa0without properly registering them, you can get in trouble. One kind of trouble that you can get in is that the SEC can sue you. Another kind of trouble that you can get in is that the people who\xc2\xa0buy the securities can demand their money back. If you sell people stock for $20 per share, and then a year later they notice that you did not properly register the offering, and the stock is now at $5, you have to give them their $20 back. (If the stock is at $30, they can keep it; they don\xe2\x80\x99t have to take their money back.)\xc2\xa0This creates a lot of bad risk for you, which you can avoid by filing the right forms.

When the company is a startup selling stock\xc2\xa0to the public for the first time, the\xc2\xa0registration process is called an \xe2\x80\x9cinitial public offering\xe2\x80\x9d and is a big deal. When the company is a giant bank that sells dozens of securities every day, it is not. It files a \xe2\x80\x9cshelf registration statement,\xe2\x80\x9d letting it sell a large\xc2\xa0amount of a wide range of securities over time, and then each day it sells some more securities \xe2\x80\x9coff that shelf,\xe2\x80\x9d and when it has sold all of the securities covered by the shelf registration statement then it files a new one and starts over again. And it is all pretty administrative and boring.

The bank uses the shelf to sell a range of securities: bonds and notes to fund its general activities, structured notes (basically customized derivatives wrapped in bonds of the bank and sold to rich people), and (at Barclays) exchange-traded products that give customers exposure to things like oil prices or the VIX volatility index. Every day the bank needs funding, and customers want to do trades, and some of those trades come in the form of securities issued off the shelf.

Normally the SEC makes this process very easy for big well-known companies. A \xe2\x80\x9cwell-known seasoned issuer,\xe2\x80\x9d or WKSI, under US securities law can file shelf registration statements that don\xe2\x80\x99t even specify a maximum amount of securities, and they are automatically \xe2\x80\x9ceffective\xe2\x80\x9d: The company can just sell securities immediately, as soon as it files the registration statement, without any SEC review. The WKSI standards are in SEC Rule 405 and Instruction I.A on Form S-3 , and the basic requirements are that you have been public for at least a year, you have a stock market capitalization of at least $700 million, and your annual and quarterly SEC reports are up-to-date. The intuition is that if you are a big company that has been public for a while, you can just say \xe2\x80\x9chey we\xe2\x80\x99re selling some securities,\xe2\x80\x9d and then you do it, without a lot of formalities.

Barclays is a big company that has been public for a while, so it was a WKSI, and it used its WKSI status to sell lots of structured notes and exchange-traded products every day.\xc2\xa0But there is one more requirement for being a WKSI, which is that you can\xe2\x80\x99t have committed any securities fraud in the last three years. [2] It is a\xc2\xa0fact of life\xc2\xa0that big\xc2\xa0banks are constantly getting in trouble for some form or other of securities fraud. At every one of the large legacy investment banks, somebody in some division is committing securities fraud right now . But the WKSI rules really are intended to let big public companies issue securities with a minimum of fuss, and nobody needs that more than a bank selling new notes every day. And in general the banks are not getting in trouble for fraudulently issuing structured notes off their WKSI shelf registration statements. ( Sometimes , though!) If someone on some mortgage-bond trading desk does some light fraud, it is odd to punish the exchange-traded-notes people by making it more inconvenient for them to register securities.

For a long time, the SEC dealt with this by granting \xe2\x80\x9cWKSI\xc2\xa0waivers\xe2\x80\x9d to banks pretty liberally: Each time a bank got caught doing something bad, it would confess its\xc2\xa0sins and pay a big fine, and then the SEC would say \xe2\x80\x9cokay but you still get to be a WKSI.\xe2\x80\x9d And the banks could keep issuing securities efficiently.

At some point this became controversial, for reasons that I have never understood , and\xc2\xa0people objected that the SEC was giving out too many of these waivers. So in 2015 the SEC collected some fines and\xc2\xa0gave out some waivers for foreign-exchange manipulation, and SEC Commissioner Kara Stein dissented , noting that the SEC was granting \xe2\x80\x9cBarclays its third WKSI waiver since 2007.\xe2\x80\x9d (UBS AG was on its seventh waiver and JPMorgan Chase & Co. its sixth.) The next year Barclays got its fourth WKSI waiver , for a $361 million settlement over some bad dark-pool stuff.

In May 2017 Barclays signed a fairly run-of-the-mill settlement with the SEC in which it agreed to pay $97 million for overcharging some clients for mutual funds. Securities fraud, sure, why not. [3] This one, though, was finally a bridge too far, and it did not get a WKSI waiver. I don\xe2\x80\x99t know why this one was the one.\xc2\xa0But after that Barclays stopped being a WKSI. [4]

This meant that instead of occasionally filing shelf registration statements saying \xe2\x80\x9chey we\xe2\x80\x99re gonna sell some securities, look out,\xe2\x80\x9d Barclays had to occasionally file shelf registration statements saying \xe2\x80\x9chey we\xe2\x80\x99re gonna sell $_______ of securities, look out.\xe2\x80\x9d And in the summer of\xc2\xa02019, Barclays filed a shelf registration statement saying that it would sell up to $20,761,600,000 of securities over the next three years. [5]

I do not know how they picked that number, which is mystifyingly precise, but I have a rough idea. [6] Barclays put together a working group of people responsible for the shelf (some lawyers, some structured-products traders, some administrative people, etc.), and the working group came up with\xc2\xa0an estimate of how many securities Barclays would need to sell over those next three years, basically by asking the heads of all the structured-product desks how much business they planned to do.

And then \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0this is crucial \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the working group came up with \xe2\x80\x9can initial allocation of fees among the trading desks accessing the 2019 Shelf.\xe2\x80\x9d See, when Barclays registered $20,761,600,000 of securities on its 2019 shelf registration statement, it had to pay a fee to the SEC, and the fee is based on how many securities it registered. WKSIs can pay their filing fees as they go : Each day you sell some new securities and pay the SEC a small fee. But non-WKSIs have to pay the fees upfront, when they file their shelf registration statement. The fee was $2,433,889.92, $2.4 million of fees for $20.8 billion of registered securities. [7]

So I assume that what happened is roughly this:

Working group lawyer: How many structured notes do you want to be able to sell over the next three years?

Structured-products desk head: I dunno, I have a good feeling about the next three years, can I have $10 billion?

Lawyer: Whatever you want, but we have to pay fees upfront, and we\xe2\x80\x99re charging those fees to the desks. So if you say $10 billion then we\xe2\x80\x99ll deduct $1.17 million from your revenue this year.

Desk head: Oh then one billion.

The incentive\xc2\xa0to say a large number is that you have no idea how many securities you\xe2\x80\x99ll sell over the next three years, you\xe2\x80\x99ll try to sell a lot, and it is better to have more flexibility than less. The incentive to say a small number is that you have to pay a 0.01% fee. You don\xe2\x80\x99t become the head of an exchange-traded-products desk by paying out 0.01% fees carelessly.

So Barclays registered exactly $20,761,600,000 of securities in summer 2019, and naturally someone then started a spreadsheet and kept careful track of how many securities it issued each day, and matched that calculation up against the $20,761,600,000 cap, and when the total issuance got reasonably close to $20,761,600,000, that person alerted senior lawyers and desk heads so that they could get to work on a new shelf registration statement well before they ran out of capacity on the old one. Right? Obviously? This is just a routine thing that you have to do: If you have registered a finite number of securities, you keep track of how many you have issued, so you don\xe2\x80\x99t run out of room on your registration statement.

This is so routine, so obvious, that apparently everyone at Barclays assumed that someone else was doing it, and no one actually did. And then one day someone in finance asked someone in legal\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9chey do you have that spreadsheet with the shelf issuances,\xe2\x80\x9d and the lawyer was like \xe2\x80\x9cno let me see who has that, I\xe2\x80\x99ll get right back to you,\xe2\x80\x9d and then a hole opened up in the earth and swallowed all of them:

On March 8, 2022, a member of Group Treasury reached out to the member of the legal department who had been part of the Working Group, inquiring as to how many securities\xc2\xa0remained available to be offered and sold off of the 2019 Shelf because Group Treasury was planning on doing a sale of corporate debt securities.

Over the course of that day and the next, various BBPLC personnel attempted to calculate the cumulative amount of securities offered and sold from the 2019 Shelf in order to determine the amount of securities that remained available for sale. Over the course of these efforts, it became clear to all involved that there was no internal control in place to track in real time the amount of securities offered and sold against the amount of securities registered.

On or around March 9, 2022, BBPLC personnel concluded that securities had been offered and sold in excess of what had been registered on the 2019 Shelf. Shortly thereafter, BBPLC halted new offers and sales of securities from the 2019 Shelf and, on March 14, 2022, alerted regulators about the over-issuance and disclosed to the market that BBPLC did not have sufficient issuance capacity to support further sales from inventory and any further issuances of certain ETNs.

I don\xe2\x80\x99t think I\xe2\x80\x99ve ever read a worse paragraph than that middle paragraph.\xc2\xa0I don\xe2\x80\x99t think that you could write horror fiction scarier than that. This year for Halloween I am going to go as It Becoming Clear to All Involved\xc2\xa0That There Was No Internal Control in Place to Track in Real Time the Amount of Securities Offered and Sold Against the Amount of Securities Registered. I think I would rather be eaten by a werewolf than have that become clear to me.

That block quote is from an SEC order against Barclays , because last week Barclays settled with the SEC again , this time for selling unregistered securities. It registered $20.8 billion of securities, and overshot that by roughly $16.37 billion. Just not particularly close at all. It is illegal to sell unregistered securities, and so Barclays paid the SEC a $200 million fine.

It also separately had to make a \xe2\x80\x9crescission offer,\xe2\x80\x9d meaning that it had to\xc2\xa0contact everyone who bought those extra securities and say \xe2\x80\x9chey, if you want your money back, let us know.\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0Holders of roughly $7.7 billion of those securities demanded their money back, which cost Barclays hundreds of millions more dollars. It does not seem to have disclosed an exact number, but in July it estimated the number at about \xc2\xa31.6 billion, and last week (after the rescission offer was completed) it confirmed that the results were \xe2\x80\x9cmaterially in line\xe2\x80\x9d with that number. [8]

In March, when Barclays discovered this oopsie, it notified the market and estimated that it would cost about\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa3450 million in rescission, but that number has grown over time. The intuition is roughly: If you bought a structured note or exchange-traded product\xc2\xa0from Barclays in late 2021 or early 2022, you were making some sort of bet in which Barclays would give you more than your money back\xc2\xa0if something happened (often: if the market was up) but Barclays would give you less than your money back if something else happened (often: if the market was down). If Barclays gave you more than your money back, you\xe2\x80\x99re happy, and you did not take the rescission offer. If Barclays gave you less than your money back, you were sad, and you did take the rescission offer. Between March and now, markets went down a lot, so more people are sad and want rescission.

When this came out in March, we talked about it here . I want to go back to two things I said at the time. First, my assumptions about how this happened:

If some junior person in the internal legal team leaves, and forgets to pass along the \xe2\x80\x9cshelfcapacityleft_07.xls\xe2\x80\x9d spreadsheet to her successor, and it stops being updated \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0then, uh. \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0You just keep bopping along as though everything was fine, and then one day a new junior analyst starts on the legal team and finds the \xe2\x80\x9cshelfcapacityleft_07.xls\xe2\x80\x9d spreadsheet in a folder and asks the vice president \xe2\x80\x9chey what is this,\xe2\x80\x9d and the VP looks, and she realizes what it is, and they check the math 20 times because it seems too horrible to be true, and then they all leave for the bar at noon because it is not a fixable error and they will miss each other when they\xe2\x80\x99re all fired tomorrow. \xe2\x80\xa6

I hope that they will eventually publish the full report and we can find out what the actual file name of \xe2\x80\x9cshelfcapacityleft_07.xls\xe2\x80\x9d was and when it was last updated. Perhaps we will learn the name of the junior analyst in the legal department responsible for updating the spreadsheet, and which law school she left for when she stopped updating it.

I feel like I captured\xc2\xa0the general feel of it, but it did not occur to me that there was never a spreadsheet at all . It\xe2\x80\x99s not that they stopped updating it, it\xe2\x80\x99s that it never existed. Not great!

Second, my assumptions about how the SEC would react:

It is not a fixable error because the rules are harsh, and there is no no-harm-no-foul rule for illegal securities offerings. If you sell securities without a valid registration statement \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0as Barclays did \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0then you have to offer to buy the securities back at the price you sold them for. If the securities were structured notes and ETNs \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0that is, bets that some index will go up or down, or weird exotic options bets \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and if you don\xe2\x80\x99t catch the mistake for a whole year, then some of them will have gone down a lot. You sold them for $100 and now they are worth $50 and you have to buy them back at $100. (Others will have gone up a lot, but you don\xe2\x80\x99t get to buy those back at par: You have to offer to rescind all of your sales, but the holders can decline.) You can call up the SEC and say \xe2\x80\x9cwe messed up a little bit but no one was harmed, sorry,\xe2\x80\x9d and the SEC will say \xe2\x80\x9cah yes we see, we understand, no one was harmed and you didn\xe2\x80\x99t mean it,\xe2\x80\x9d and they will feel sorry for you, but they will still make you offer to buy back all the ETNs. And then you\xe2\x80\x99ll lose 450 million pounds.

Well. I mean. That\xe2\x80\x99s what I would say, if I were the SEC! I would say \xe2\x80\x9clook yes there\xe2\x80\x99s no way around it, this is going to cost you hundreds of millions of dollars to buy back all those structured notes and exchange-traded products that you sold. I\xe2\x80\x99m sorry, it\xe2\x80\x99s very unfair: The speculators who bought those notes made a bet, and they lost, and they get their money back due to a random administrative windfall. You didn\xe2\x80\x99t actually deceive or defraud them in any way, and your dumb administrative mistake didn\xe2\x80\x99t harm them, but they do get to take advantage of it to get their money back, sorry.\xe2\x80\x9d

The actual SEC fined Barclays $200 million on top of the rescission costs , and said this :

"This case highlights why it is essential for firms like Barclays to have robust internal controls over their offers and sales of securities," said Gurbir S. Grewal, Director of the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s Division of Enforcement. "While we acknowledge Barclays\xe2\x80\x99 efforts to identify, disclose and remediate this conduct, the control deficiencies and the scope of the conduct at issue here was simply staggering. The time for other firms employing similar shelf registrations to take notice and improve their internal compliance and control functions is now."

Just unbelievably harsh! Sure yes this was very bad , but the only victim of it was Barclays; the investors who bought unregistered securities were not deceived in any way, and many of them ended up with a random windfall. Sure yes Barclays\xe2\x80\x99s shareholders suffered by having to pay out that random windfall, but it\xe2\x80\x99s not like fining them $200 million more makes that any better.

Not legal advice but

If your older brother does some crime, and he collects his loot in Bitcoin, and the US government seizes his Bitcoin, and you steal the Bitcoin back out from under the government\xe2\x80\x99s nose, and you convert the Bitcoin into dollars, should you put the dollars into a bathtub and roll around in them\xc2\xa0while women dance around you and your buddy takes pictures on your\xc2\xa0phone? I am not your lawyer and I am not going to give you legal advice, though I think you can easily imagine the look your lawyer would give you if you asked her this question. I will say though that if you ask your buddies this\xc2\xa0question, they would probably say \xe2\x80\x9coh absolutely yes that sounds like a good time,\xe2\x80\x9d and I cannot really fault them.\xc2\xa0Rolling around in a bathtub of money that you stole from the government really is funny. Anyway here\xe2\x80\x99s Bloomberg\xe2\x80\x99s David Voreacos with one of the great first paragraphs of financial journalism :

Gary Harmon grinned as he lounged in a bathtub full of dollar bills surrounded by scantily clad women. The moment, captured in a photo on his cellphone, could be part of his undoing. To US prosecutors, it\xe2\x80\x99s evidence that he suddenly came into a lot of money.

I will buy what they are selling on this point. If you show me a picture of a man sitting in a bathtub of dollar bills, [9] I am definitely going to think \xe2\x80\x9cthis man acquired that money suddenly.\xe2\x80\x9d You don\xe2\x80\x99t grind away at a steady job for 40 years and then have your retirement party in a bathtub full of your pension. A bathtub of money really does indicate suddenness. Voreacos goes on:

The prosecutors accuse Harmon of a very unusual crime: remotely swiping Bitcoin stored on a computer device the government had already seized in another case, brought against his older brother, Larry. As authorities watched helplessly, 713 digital tokens\xe2\x80\x94then worth almost $5 million\xe2\x80\x94were somehow spirited away from the \xe2\x80\x9chardware wallet\xe2\x80\x9d they were holding in an evidence locker.

Larry Harmon, who\xe2\x80\x99s since pleaded guilty to laundering $311 million through crypto transactions, swore up and down he wasn\xe2\x80\x99t involved in the disappearing act. Instead, Larry, 39, pointed the finger at Gary, 30, and ultimately helped to nail him. Gary is in federal jail in Washington, D.C., awaiting trial, and Larry is free on bail near Akron. The cases of the Harmons\xe2\x80\x94literal crypto bros\xe2\x80\x94show how the IRS and the FBI are succeeding in collecting evidence but still face challenges on the blockchain frontier. Authorities had to track digital money moving through a tangle of anonymous accounts to connect it to Larry. When they tried to seize it, they faced a problem: How do you put a fence around a quicksilver asset such as Bitcoin?

Colloquially you can say that you have Bitcoin \xe2\x80\x9con\xe2\x80\x9d a hardware wallet or other computer storage device, like the guy in Wales who threw his in a garbage dump and keeps getting publicity for his schemes to dig it up. But really the hardware wallet just stores your private keys to one or more Bitcoin addresses, and having those on a hardware wallet doesn\xe2\x80\x99t stop you from also storing them somewhere else \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0on another hardware wallet, in your iPhone notes app, on a Post-it note, by\xc2\xa0memorizing them (or by memorizing a seed phrase that can be used to generate the keys), etc.\xc2\xa0The lesson for the government is that if you arrest someone for Bitcoin crime and you seize his hardware wallet, you have not seized his Bitcoin; you have just seized a hard drive, and he \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0or his brother, or anyone else \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0might have other ways to access the Bitcoins. The way you seize the Bitcoins is by getting his private keys \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0from the hardware wallet, or by asking him, or whatever \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and then transferring them to an address that you control, where you can be pretty sure that he and his friends do not have the keys.

Whistles, whistles

I wrote in August about Elon Musk\xe2\x80\x99s efforts to get out of buying Twitter Inc.:

The situation is that the richest person in the world, Elon Musk, has $44 billion riding on the question of whether Twitter Inc. is doing a massive long-running fraud on its users, its advertisers and its shareholders. Musk has claimed that it is, with no evidence at all, just a vague feeling that he has. If he\xe2\x80\x99s right and there is a massive fraud, then he can get out of his agreement to buy Twitter for about $44 billion. If he\xe2\x80\x99s wrong, he has to buy Twitter.

This situation is extremely well known. There are incentives. If you have something to do with Twitter, or you know something about Twitter, or you are in the business of counting Twitter bots, then you should take a few minutes and ask yourself: Well, do I know anything about Twitter doing any massive frauds? If you do \xe2\x80\xa6 look, I don\xe2\x80\x99t know exactly what you can do with that. I am not at all sure that you can actually go to Elon Musk and be like \xe2\x80\x9chey I\xe2\x80\x99ve got a pile of fraud documents, they\xe2\x80\x99re yours for $1 billion.\xe2\x80\x9d (He could send you a subpoena and get them for free?) Even if you did strike a deal with him, he\xe2\x80\x99s notoriously bad about sticking to his deals. Still. I just have a vague general sense that saving the richest person in the world $44 billion is a good career move.

So you should expect that, over the next month or two, as Musk\xe2\x80\x99s trial date with Twitter approaches, people will be popping up to say \xe2\x80\x9coh sure Twitter is a fraud, massive fraud, totally, I have proof, Elon, call me.\xe2\x80\x9d

Well, that case goes to trial this month, and here\xe2\x80\x99s this :

Another Twitter whistleblower is considering taking the stand when Elon Musk faces the company in court over his $44 billion buyout deal later in two weeks \xe2\x80\x94 and he may have a few things to say about bots, The Post has learned. ...

The would-be whistleblower, a former Twitter employee, claims to have been involved in an internal report several years ago that supposedly concluded that at least 30% of Twitter\xe2\x80\x99s daily active users are automated spam accounts.

\xe2\x80\x9cTwitter execs laughed when they were told about the report and said, \xe2\x80\x98We have always had a bot problem,\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d the potential whistleblower said in an interview with The Post. \xe2\x80\xa6

The potential whistleblower said that Twitter\xe2\x80\x99s chosen metric to measure bots \xe2\x80\x94 called Monetizable Daily Active Users, or mDAUs \xe2\x80\x94 is overly narrow and fails to fully capture the extent of the site\xe2\x80\x99s spam problem. He said Twitter may not be lying when it says that less than 5% of its mDAUs are bots, but takes issue with using mDAU as a measurement in the first place and claims the company has not been transparent with investors.

Yes look if he testifies that Twitter\xe2\x80\x99s securities filings were accurate, but he didn\xe2\x80\x99t like them, then that won\xe2\x80\x99t help Musk very much will it. The problem here is that the incentives are so large and well-known that they will attract a lot of people who are eager to help but have nothing to offer.

Things happen

Credit Suisse\xe2\x80\x99s Options Worsen as Markets Mayhem Takes Toll.\xc2\xa0How big is the capital hole at Credit Suisse? Kwarteng Gave BOE Permission for \xc2\xa3100 Billion of Bond Buying . Fund Managers, Regulators Wrestle Over Plans to Tighten ESG Rules . China\xe2\x80\x99s property crash : \xe2\x80\x98a slow-motion financial crisis.\xe2\x80\x99 Poshmark to Sell Itself for Less Than Half Its IPO Price to Korea\xe2\x80\x99s Naver. Nikola Founder Trevor Milton Won\xe2\x80\x99t Testify in Fraud Trial. Theranos Founder Elizabeth Holmes Gets Hearing on New Trial. Bankrupt Crypto Firm Celsius Sets Dates for Auction of Assets. Crypto Could Threaten Financial System , Federal Risk Panel Warns. PwC sees opportunity to poach staff during EY split . Software Robots Are Gaining Ground in White-Collar Office World. Elon Musk Sets Off Uproar in Ukraine by Tweeting His \xe2\x80\x98Peace\xe2\x80\x99 Plan . \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s about addressing a feeling I think a lot of people relate to, and turning that feeling that already exists of guilt or anxiety into a sense of freedom, or empowerment or joy .\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cCan you buy Twitter and then delete it , please!? xx.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cVerified users are without question the worst part of any mainstream platform and if you want to imagine a world without online anonymity, go tell me about the incredible original content trending on LinkedIn right now.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cThe Onion files this brief to protect its continued ability to create fiction that may ultimately merge into reality .\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cIf you do what you hate, the money will chase you down a dark alleyway and throttle you .\xe2\x80\x9d Adult Happy Meals .

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] I am just going to call the whole thing \xe2\x80\x9cBarclays,\xe2\x80\x9d and Barclays Plc is the top-level holding company. In fact various Barclays entities are involved here, and in particular last week\xe2\x80\x99s settlement is with Barclays Plc and Barclays Bank Plc; Barclays Bank Plc is the filer of the relevant registration statements.

[2] This is in the \xe2\x80\x9cineligible issuer\xe2\x80\x9d definition in Rule 405 , which includes among other things an exclusion if \xe2\x80\x9cWithin the past three years \xe2\x80\xa6 the issuer or any entity that at the time was a subsidiary of the issuer was made the subject of any judicial or administrative decree or order arising out of a governmental action that: \xe2\x80\xa6 Determines that the person violated the anti-fraud provisions of the federal securities laws.\xe2\x80\x9d

[3] \xe2\x80\x9cAs a result of the conduct described above, Barclays Capital willfully violated Section 206(2) of the Advisers Act, which prohibits an investment adviser from engaging in any transaction, practice, or course of business that operates as a fraud or deceit upon a client or prospective client,\xe2\x80\x9d says the 2017 SEC order .

[4] For three years. It\xe2\x80\x99s a WKSI again as of May 2022, and now has a WKSI shelf .

[5] For simplicity I am skipping over another dumb detail of this story, which is that Barclays overshot its issuance on a *previous* shelf registration statement (a 2018 conversion of its WKSI shelf into a non-WKSI limited shelf). That was a smaller dollar amount and a less dramatic discovery, but same basic problem.

[6] Obviously I\xe2\x80\x99m relying on, and quoting, last week\xe2\x80\x99s SEC order .

[7] See the cover page of the registration statement . The SEC\xe2\x80\x99s filing fee rates change each year; the current fee is $110.20 per million dollars registered.

[8] For some reason the SEC says that its settlement is \xe2\x80\x9c$361 million,\xe2\x80\x9d consisting of a $200 million fine plus $161 million of \xe2\x80\x9cdisgorgement,\xe2\x80\x9d with the latter \xe2\x80\x9cdeemed satisfied by its offer of rescission.\xe2\x80\x9d But that does not seem to mean that Barclays paid $161 million of rescission \xe2\x80\x94 it seems to have paid a lot more. Note by the way that the rescission offer was for $17.7 billion, which covers both the $16.4 billion overshoot on the 2019 shelf and also the smaller overshoot on the earlier shelf.

[9] Singles, mind you; it looks like there is maybe $150 in this picture. So not *great* evidence.
